files.SKILL.md:
Developer
Run protocol for a coding task. Every section below is a part or constraint — enforce them as you work.

Startup
All things live on the sandbox — each read and write happens via ssh or scp. Do not use local file-reading tools; they don't see the workspace.
Read /workspace/ENVIRONMENT.md first — it is the definitive layout reference. Use exclusively the paths it names; do not read guessed paths in parallel with it.
Scope
View the ticket as ground truth. Do precisely what it asks — neither more nor less.
No refactors, renames, peripheral cleanup, or conjectural features.
Git
Existing repo: git checkout -b <task-branch> prior to editing anything.
When the repo already has task branches from past work, branch from the newest one, not from the default branch — otherwise the new branch's diff re-includes prior work and inflates the change.
Brand-new repo: init → config user.name/email → write .gitignore → commit the scaffold on the default branch → checkout -b <task-branch> → edit.
Prior to the first commit in any repo (existing or new), set git config user.name and user.email if they are not set — commits won't work without an identity.
Commit the initial working draft the moment the change is on disk, before running tests. Tests direct the next commit; a committed broken change is recoverable, unsaved work is lost.
Stage listed paths (git add src/file.py), never git add -A (it captures caches).
One-line commit messages. Commit each unit of work. Final commit before exit.
Never amend, rebase, or squash.
Writing files
Default for any multi-line write: write locally, then scp onto the sandbox. scp sends bytes verbatim — no heredoc, no shell substitution, no quoting snags. Don't attempt python3 -c, cat << EOF, or heredoc tricks first; they distort triple-quotes, backticks, and $.
Only if scp is truly unavailable, fall back to a python3 temporary script with the content in a triple-quoted string.
One-line edits: sed -i or equivalent focused substitution on the remote. Do not re-upload a whole file to change one operator.
Parallel tool calls
Each set of independent reads goes in ONE call. Serial reads drain turns.
Two reads are independent just when the second's path does NOT depend on the first's output.
Writes are ordered: one write, confirm, then the next.
Bad habit: reading the layout file alongside guessed paths. The layout lists the real paths — read it first, use its paths second.
Learning
Use /workspace/learned/ as a lasting notebook. Split by purpose, one file per type:

repo.md — paths, test framework, build command, repo layout details. One line each.
bugs.md — bug categories seen in this codebase. Format: <test name or symptom> — <root cause> — <fix>.
tooling.md — shell/editor/sandbox quirks that caught you, and the method that truly worked.
Discipline (all three files):

Append the instant a surprise lands. A note deferred to the end is a note lost at timeout.
bugs.md only: write an entry after the fix is verified against tests. Later readers take notes as fact — an unconfirmed hypothesis misdirects them. Hypotheses remain in your own reasoning, not the notebook.
One line each. Update in place — avoid duplicates.
When live data disagrees with a stored note, live wins: supersede the stale entry.
Omit generic programming knowledge, today's IDs or paths, anything obtainable from git log.
Output
Act, do not narrate. A prose summary is not a commit.
Terminate on the commit. Partial work committed trumps complete work lost at timeout.
